elements.sent-2411-001: The existence of {all small-amplitude Wilton ripple solutions of the Kawahara equation is proven.}
elements.sent-2411-002: These are periodic, traveling-wave solutions that bifurcate from a two-dimensional nullspace spanned by two distinct, co-propagating cosine waves.
elements.sent-2411-003: In contrast with previous results, the proof, which relies on a carefully constructed Lyapunov-Schmidt reduction, implies the existence of all small-amplitude Wilton ripples of the Kawahara equation, of which there are countably infinite.
elements.sent-2411-004: Though this result pertains only to the Kawahara equation, the method of proof likely extends to most nonlinear dispersive equations admitting Wilton ripple solutions.
elements.sent-2411-005: We consider the Kawahara (or super-KdV) equation \begin{equation}u_t = \alpha u_{xxx} + \beta u_{5x} + \sigma \left(u^2\right)_x, \label{kEqn1}\end{equation} where $\alpha, \beta,$ and $\sigma$ are real, nonzero parameters.
elements.sent-2411-006: First derived in \cite{kawahara1972 , the Kawahara equation models shallow water gravity-capillary waves near a critical value of the Bond number, a nondimensional quantity that compares gravity and surface tension.}
elements.sent-2411-007: Owing to the competition between these forces, the Kawahara equation admits several interesting and physically-relevant solutions; see, for instance, \cite{alyousef2022new -\cite{assas2009new and \cite{mancas2019traveling,sirendaoreji2004new,ye2022all .}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Of importance in this work is a class of periodic, traveling-wave solutions called the Wilton ripples, which have a particularly rich history in the context of water waves\footnote{More recently, these solutions have found practical relevance in the context of magnetohydrodynamics \cite{zakaria2004wilton .. \cite{wilton1915lxxii .}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: To motivate the mathematical behavior of Wilton ripples, we begin by searching for the periodic, traveling-wave solutions of the Kawahara equation.
elements.sent-2411-010: To this effect, we move to a traveling frame $x \rightarrow x - ct$ so that \eqref{kEqn1 becomes \begin{equation}u_t = cu_x + \alpha u_{xxx} + \beta u_{5x} + \sigma \left( \sigma^2 \right)_x.\label{kEqn_trav}\end{equation} If $c$ is chosen to match the velocity of the traveling waves, then $u_t = 0$, and we can integrate \eqref{kEqn_trav once in $x$, yielding \begin{equation}cu + \alpha u_{xx} + \beta u_{4x} + \sigma u^2 = I, \label{kEqn_noT_I}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{I \in \mathbb{R}$ is a constant of integration.}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: By exploiting a Galilean symmetry of \eqref{kEqn1 , one can show that there exist boosts $u \rightarrow u + \xi_1$ and $c \rightarrow c + \xi_2$ of \eqref{kEqn_noT_I such that $\mathcal{I = 0$ without loss of generality.}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: What remains is to study the $2\pi/\kappa$-periodic solutions of \begin{equation}cu + \alpha u_{xx} + \beta u_{4x} + \sigma u^2 = 0,\end{equation} for all $\kappa > 0$.
elements.sent-2411-013: In fact, by rescaling variables according to $x \rightarrow \kappa^{-1 x$, $u \rightarrow \alpha k^2 \sigma^{-1 u$, $c \rightarrow \alpha k^2 c$, and $\beta \rightarrow \alpha \kappa^{-2 \beta$, we can instead investigate the $2\pi$-periodic solutions of \begin{equation}cu + u_{xx} + \beta u_{4x} + u^2 = 0,\label{kEqn_final}\end{equation} without loss of generality.}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Furthermore, because \eqref{kEqn_final is autonomous in $x$ and satisfies the discrete symmetry $x \rightarrow -x$, we need only consider its $2\pi$-periodic, even solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-015: If we further restrict these solutions to small-amplitude, \eqref{kEqn_final effectively linearizes to \begin{equation}cu + u_{xx} + \beta u_{4x} = 0. \label{lin_stokes}\end{equation} At first glance, it appears that the only\footnote{It is also possible that $\cos(nx)$ solves \eqref{lin_stokes when $c = n^2 - \beta n^4$ for any $n \in \mathbb{N}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: However, by folding $n$ into the definition of $\kappa$ before rescaling $x$, one can restrict to the $n = 1$ case without loss of generality.
elements.sent-2411-017: However, if $\beta = 1/(1+K^2)$ for any $K \in \mathbb{N \setminus \{1\}$, then $u = a\cos(x) + b\cos(Kx)$ satisfies \eqref{lin_stokes with $c = 1-\beta$ for all $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Hence, depending on the value of $\beta$, we have two classes of small-amplitude, periodic traveling-wave solutions of the Kawahara equation: \begin{center.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=15cm]{figure_1.png} \caption{(a) A Stokes wave of the Kawahara equation for $\beta = 1/2$, (b) A Wilton ripple of the Kawahara equation for $\beta = 1/5$, and (c) A Wilton ripple of the Kawahara equation for $\beta = 1/10$. In all plots, the amplitude parameter $a = 0.01$. The blue dots are numerically computed wave profiles using the continuation method presented in \cite{trichtchenko2018stability}. The red curves are the leading-order asymptotic behavior of the wave profiles according to Theorem 1 below. The wave profiles are normalized by $a$ for ease of comparisons.}
elements.sent-2411-019: When $\beta \neq 1/(1+K^2)$, the periodic traveling-wave solutions bifurcate from a single cosine wave.
elements.sent-2411-020: For $|a|\ll 1$, these solutions take the form \begin{equation}u = a\cos(x) + u_r(x;a) \quad and \quad c = 1-\beta + c_r(a),\end{equation} where $u_r(x;a)$ is a smooth, $2\pi$-periodic function in $x$ that is real analytic in $a$ with $||u_r(x;a)||_{L^2 = \mathcal{O}\left(a^2\right)$ as $a \rightarrow 0$ and $c_r(a)$ is an even, real-analytic function in $a$ such that $|c_r(a)| = \mathcal{O}\left(a^2\right)$ as $a \rightarrow 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-021: We call such solutions the Stokes waves of the Kawahara equation by analogy with the corresponding solutions of the surface gravity wave equations \cite{stokes1847theory .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Their existence for sufficiently small $a$ was proven in \cite{haragus2006spectral and their stability discussed in \cite{creedon2021high,haragus2006spectral,maspero2024full,trichtchenko2018stability .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: The profile of a typical Stokes wave solution is provided in Figure 1(a).
elements.sent-2411-024: When $\beta = 1/(1+K^2)$, the periodic traveling-wave solutions bifurcate from a superposition of two co-propagating cosine waves.
elements.sent-2411-025: These are the Wilton ripples of the Kawahara equation, named by analogy with the corresponding solutions of the gravity-capillary wave equations \cite{wilton1915lxxii .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Unlike the Stokes waves, Wilton ripples arise from a two-dimensional bifurcation problem, and as a consequence, their expansion for small $a$ is different than that of the Stokes waves.
elements.sent-2411-027: Indeed, based on the formal asymptotic expansions derived in \cite{haupt1988modeling,langer2024wilton , the Wilton ripple solutions of the Kawahara equation take the form \begin{equation}u = a\Big(\cos(x) + b(a)\cos(Kx)\Big) + u_r(x;a) \quad and \quad c = 1-\beta + c_r(a), \label{wilton_exp}\end{equation} where $b(a)$ is a real analytic function of $a$, $u_r(x;a)$ is a smooth, $2\pi$-periodic function of $x$ that is real analytic in $a$, and $c_r(a)$ is a real analytic function of $a$.}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}u = a\Big(\cos(x) + b(a)\cos(Kx)\Big) + u_r(x;a) \quad and \quad c = 1-\beta + c_r(a), \label{wilton_exp}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-028: The leading-order behaviors of $b(a)$, $u_r(x;a)$, and $c_r(a)$ as $a \rightarrow 0$ depend on $K$ according to Theorem 1 below.
elements.sent-2411-029: Profiles of typical Wilton ripple solutions are provided in Figures 1(b) and 1(c).
elements.sent-2411-030: Nearly all of these proofs, however, restrict to the simplest possible ripple that bifurcates from a superposition of $\cos(x)$ and $\cos(2x)$.
elements.sent-2411-031: In the context of the Kawahara equation, this is the special case when $K = 2$.
elements.sent-2411-032: A notable exception is \cite{ehrnstrom2019bifurcation , which adopts a new framework for studying Wilton ripples of a gravity-capillary Whitham equation.}
elements.sent-2411-033: In this framework, both $a$ and $b$ are fixed, and the remaining parameters in the governing equation of the Wilton ripples\footnote{This would be $\beta$ in the Kawahara equation.}
elements.sent-2411-034: As a consequence, one can prove the existence of a two-dimensional ``sheet'' of Wilton ripples parameterized by $a$ and $b$.
elements.sent-2411-035: Recent numerical and formal asymptotic investigations by \cite{akersbanff have applied this new framework to the Kawahara equation, and the results suggest that the Wilton ripples discussed herein are embedded in this sheet of solutions (at least for $K = 2$ and $K = 3$).}
elements.sent-2411-036: However, no rigorous proof of this embedding has been established.
elements.sent-2411-037: Thus, the existence of Wilton ripples for all $K \in \mathbb{N \setminus \{1\}$ remains an open problem.}
elements.sent-2411-038: It is the objective of this work to prove this fact.
elements.sent-2411-039: To the author's knowledge, this is the first proof of its kind to demonstrate the existence of all Wilton ripple solutions of a nonlinear dispersive equation.
elements.sent-2411-040: In contrast to many previous existence proofs, which rely on Banach fixed point arguments or the method of majorants, this proof relies on a carefully constructed Lyapunov-Schmidt reduction to prove that each Wilton ripple depends analytically on a small-amplitude parameter.
elements.sent-2411-041: It is anticipated that this part of the proof extends with minimal changes to Wilton ripples of any nonlinear dispersive equation, including the gravity-capillary water wave equations.
elements.sent-2411-042: However, to finish the existence proof, one must show that the function $b(a)$ in \eqref{wilton_exp does not vanish identically for each $K \in \mathbb{N \setminus \{1\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: In order to show this, we need high-order asymptotic expansions of all Wilton ripples.
elements.sent-2411-044: For general nonlinear dispersive equations, these expansions have not been carried out, but recent work by \cite{langer2024wilton has derived these expansions for the Kawahara equation.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Using these expansions, we can prove our main result:.
elements.sent-2411-046: If $K = 2$ (so that $\beta = 1/5$), there exist two $a$-parameter families of solutions $u_{+ (x;a)$ and $u_{- (x;a)$ that solve \eqref{kEqn_final for sufficiently small $a \in \mathbb{R}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: The solutions have velocity \begin{equation}c_{\pm} = \frac{4}{5} - \frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}\tilde{c_r}^{\pm}(a),\end{equation} respectively, where $\tilde{c_r ^{\pm (a)$ are real analytic functions such that $\tilde{c_r ^{\pm (0) = \pm 1.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: If $K = 3$ (so that $\beta = 1/10$), there exist three a-parameter families of solutions $u_\sigma(x;a)$ for $\sigma \in \{1,2,3\}$ that solve \eqref{kEqn_final for sufficiently small $a \in \mathbb{R}$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: The solutions have velocity \begin{equation}c_\sigma = \frac{9}{10} + a^2\tilde{c_r}^{(\sigma)}(a),\end{equation} where $\tilde{c_r ^{(\sigma) (a)$ are real analytic functions such that \begin{equation}\tilde{c_r}^{(1)}(0) \approx 4.27863 , \quad \tilde{c_r}^{(2)}(0) \approx 1.48289, \quad and \quad \tilde{c_r}^{(3)}(0) \approx 0.37396 .\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: The solutions take the form \begin{equation}u_\sigma(x;a) = a\Big(\cos(x) + \tilde{b}^{(\sigma)}(a)\cos(3x)\Big) + u_r\Big(x;a,a\tilde{b}^{(\sigma)}(a),a^2\tilde{c_r}^{(\sigma)}(a)\Big),\end{equation} where $\tilde{b ^{(\sigma) (a)$ are real analytic functions such that \begin{equation}\tilde{b}^{(1)}(0) \approx -1.78374, \quad \tilde{b}^{(2)}(0) \approx -0.54488, \quad and \quad \tilde{b}^{(3)}(0) \approx 0.59468,\end{equation} and $u_r \in H^{\infty _{per,even (\mathbb{T )$ is a real analytic function in its final three arguments.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: If $K \geq 4$ (so that $\beta = 1/(1+K^2)$), there exists one $a$-parameter family of solutions $u(x;a)$ that solves \eqref{kEqn_final for sufficiently small $a \in \mathbb{R}$.}
elements.sent-2411-052: The solutions have velocity \begin{equation}c = \frac{K^2}{K^2+1} + a^2\tilde{c_r}(a),\end{equation} where $\tilde{c_r (a)$ is a real analytic function such that \begin{equation}\tilde{c_r}(0) = \frac{(K^2+1)(5K^2-24)}{6K^2(K^2-4)}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-053: The solutions take the form \begin{equation}u(x;a) = a\Big(\cos(x) + \tilde{b}(a)\cos(Kx)\Big) + u_r\Big(x;a,a\tilde{b}(a),a^2\tilde{c_r}(a)\Big),\end{equation} where $\tilde{b (a)$ is a nonzero, real analytic function such that $\tilde{b (a) = \mathcal{O}\left(a^{K-3}\right)$ as $a \rightarrow 0$ and $u_r \in H^{\infty _{per,even (\mathbb{T )$ is a real analytic function in its final three arguments.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: On the interval $\mathbb{T = [-\pi,\pi]$, define the Hilbert space \begin{equation}H^4_{per,even}(T) := \left\{f : f(-\pi) = f(\pi), ~f(-x) = f(x), ~ and ~ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} (1+k^2)^4\left|\widehat{f}_k\right|^2 < +\infty \right\}, \label{H4}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\widehat{f}_k = F_k[f(x)] =  \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f(x)\cos(kx) dx k > 0 \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f(x)dx k = 0 ,\end{equation} are the Fourier cosine coefficients of $f$.}
elements.formula-2411-018: \begin{equation}\widehat{f}_k = F_k[f(x)] =  \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f(x)\cos(kx) dx k > 0 \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f(x)dx k = 0 ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-056: Define the nonlinear operator $F:H^4_{per,even (\mathbb{T ) \times \mathbb{R \rightarrow L^2_{per,even (\mathbb{T )$ according to \begin{equation}F(u,c) = (c+ L)u + u^2, \quad where \quad L := \partial_x^2 + \beta\partial_x^4.\label{Fdefn}\end{equation} Then, \eqref{kEqn_final becomes the operator equation \begin{equation}F(u,c) = 0. \label{opEqn}\end{equation} Over the given domain $H^4_{per,even (\mathbb{T )$, the linear operator $\mathcal{L}$ is bounded for all $c \in \mathbb{R}$ and is self-adjoint with respect to the standard $L^2(\mathbb{T )$ inner-product: \begin{equation}\left<v,w \right> = \int_{-\pi}^{\pi}v(x)w(x) dx. \label{innerProd}\end{equation} As discussed in the Introduction, $c_0+\mathcal{L}$ has a two-dimensional nullspace spanned by $\cos(x)$ and $\cos(K x)$ when $c_0 := 1-\beta$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Owing to self-adjointness, the cokernel of $c_0+\mathcal{L}$ is also spanned by $\cos(x)$ and $\cos(Kx)$, which guarantees that $c_0+\mathcal{L}$ is a Fredholm operator of index zero.
elements.sent-2411-058: In what follows, we decompose $c$ according to \begin{equation}c = c_0 + c_r, \label{cDecomp}\end{equation} where $c_r \in \mathbb{R}$ is conceived as a small correction to $c_0$.
elements.sent-2411-059: We also decompose $u \in H^4_{per,even (\mathbb{T )$ by \begin{equation}u = u_0 + u_r, \label{uDecomp}\end{equation} where $u_0 \in Null\{c_0+\mathcal{L \}$ and $u_r \in Null\{c_0+\mathcal{L _0 \}^\perp$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Substituting \eqref{cDecomp and \eqref{uDecomp into \eqref{Fdefn yields a new operator equation \begin{equation}G(u_r,a,b,c_r)= 0, \label{Gdefn}\end{equation} where $G : Null\{c_0+\mathcal{L \}^\perp \times \mathbb{R ^3 \rightarrow L^2_{per,even (\mathbb{T ) $ such that \begin{equation}G(u_r,a,b,c_r) := F\left(a\cos(x)+b\cos(Kx)+u_r,c_0+c_r\right) \nonumber ~= (c_0+L)u_r + c_r\left(a\cos(x)+b\cos(Kx)+u_r\right) + \left(a\cos(x)+b\cos(Kx)+u_r\right)^2.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: We are now ready to proceed with Lyapunov-Schmidt reduction on \eqref{Gdefn .}
elements.sent-2411-062: We introduce the projection operators $\mathcal{P}$ and $\mathcal{Q}$ according to \begin{subequations \begin{equation}Pf = \widehat{f}_1\cos(x) + \widehat{f}_K\cos(Kx), Qf = f - Pf,\end{equation} \end{subequations which are valid for all $f \in L^2_{per,even (\mathbb{T )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Here, the operator $\mathcal{P}$ projects any function in $L^2_{per,even (\mathbb{T )$ onto the cokernel of $c_0 + \mathcal{L}$, while $\mathcal{Q}$ projects onto the range of $c_0+\mathcal{L}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Applying $\mathcal{Q}$ to \eqref{Gdefn yields a new operator equation \begin{equation}A(u_r,a,b,c_r) = 0, \label{Aeqn}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}A(u_r,a,b,c_r) :=QG(u_r,a,b,c_r) = c_ru_r + Q(c_0+L)u_r + Q\left(a\cos(x)+b\cos(Kx) + u_r\right)^2. \label{Adefn}\end{equation} We call \eqref{Aeqn the auxiliary equation.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Moreover, since the operator $F$ in \eqref{Fdefn is real analytic with respect to $u$ and $c$ and the projections $\mathcal{P}$ and $\mathcal{Q}$ are bounded linear operators, $\mathcal{A}$ is real analytic with respect to $u_r$, $a$, $b$, and $c_r$.}
elements.sent-2411-067: A straightforward calculation shows that \begin{equation}\partial_{u_r} A(0,0,0) = Q(c_0+L),\end{equation} where $\partial_{u_r}$ denotes the Fr\'{e chet derivative with respect to $u_r$.}
elements.sent-2411-068: The resulting operator $\mathcal{Q (c_0+\mathcal{L ) : Null\{c_0+\mathcal{L}\}^\perp \rightarrow Range\{c_0+\mathcal{L}\}$ has bounded inverse \begin{equation}\left(Q(c_0+L)\right)^{-1}(f(x)) = \sum_{k \in N_0 \setminus \{1,K\}} \left(\frac{\widehat{f}_k}{c_0-k^2+\beta k^4} \right) \cos(kx).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-069: As a consequence, the implicit function theorem gives the existence and uniqueness of \begin{equation}u_r = u_r(x;a,b,c_r), \label{ur_soln}\end{equation} such that, for sufficiently small $a$, $b$, and $c_r$, we have (i) $u_r(x;a,b,c_r) \in Null\{c_0+\mathcal{L}\}^\perp \subset H^4_{per,even (\mathbb{T )$, (ii) $\mathcal{A (u_r(x;a,b,c_r),a,b,c_r) = 0$, (iii) $u_r(x;0,0,0) = 0$, and (iv) $u_r(x;a,b,c_r)$ is jointly real analytic with respect to $a$, $b$, and $c_r$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: By analyticity, \eqref{ur_soln admits a Taylor series expansion of the form \begin{equation}u_r(x;a,b,c_r) = u_r^{(1,0,0)}(x)a + u_r^{(0,1,0)}(x)b + u_r^{(0,0,1)}(x)c_r + u_r^{(2,0,0)}(x)a^2 + u_r^{(1,1,0)}(x)ab \nonumber \quad+ u_r^{(0,2,0)}(x) b^2 + u_r^{(0,1,1)}(x)bc_r + u_r^{(0,0,2)}(x) c_r^2 + u_r^{(1,0,1)}(x)ac_r + R_3(x;a,b,c_r), \label{ur_exp}\end{equation} where $u_r^{(i,j,k) (x)$ are explicitly computed in the Appendix and $R_3(x;a,b,c_r) \in Null\{c_0+\mathcal{L}\}^\perp \subset H^4_{per,even (\mathbb{T )$ such that \begin{equation}\Big|\Big|R_3(x;a,b,c_r)\Big|\Big|_{H^4(T)} = O\left((|a|+|b|+|c_r|)^3\right) \quad as \quad a,b,c_r \rightarrow 0;\end{equation} see \cite{chow2012methods for details.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: Applying the projection $\mathcal{P}$ to \eqref{Gdefn yields a new operator equation \begin{equation}B(u_r,a,b,c_r) = 0, \label{Beqn}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{B : Null\{c_0+\mathcal{L}\}^\perp \cross \mathbb{R ^3 \rightarrow Coker\{c_0 + \mathcal{L \}$ such that \begin{equation}B(u_r,a,b,c_r) := PG(u_r,a,b,c_r) \nonumber ~= c_r\left(a\cos(x) + b\cos(Kx) \right) + P\left(a\cos(x) + b\cos(Kx) + u_r \right)^2.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-036: \begin{equation}B(u_r,a,b,c_r) := PG(u_r,a,b,c_r) \nonumber ~= c_r\left(a\cos(x) + b\cos(Kx) \right) + P\left(a\cos(x) + b\cos(Kx) + u_r \right)^2.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-072: Substituting $u_r = u_r(x;a,b,c_r)$ into \eqref{Beqn and separating terms proportional to $\cos(x)$ and $\cos(Kx)$, we arrive at a finite-dimensional operator equation \begin{equation}\tilde{B}(a,b,c_r) = 0, \label{tildeBeqn}\end{equation} where $\tilde{B : \mathbb{R ^3 \rightarrow \mathbb{R ^2$ such that \begin{equation}\tilde{B}(a,b,c_r) =  F_1\left[\left(a\cos(x)+b\cos(Kx) + u_r(x;a,b,c_r) \right)^2\right] + ac_r F_K\left[\left(a\cos(x)+b\cos(Kx) + u_r(x;a,b,c_r) \right)^2\right]+ bc_r .\end{equation}.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-075: To this end, we let $\omega \in \{1,K\}$ and consider \begin{equation}F_{\omega}\left[\left(a\cos(x)+b\cos(Kx) + u_r(x;a,b,c_r) \right)^2 \right] = T_{\omega,1} + T_{\omega,2} + T_{\omega,3},\end{equation} where \begin{subequations \begin{equation}T_{\omega,1} = F_{\omega}\left[\left(a\cos(x)+b\cos(Kx) \right)^2 \right], \label{T1} T_{\omega,2} = F_{\omega}\left[2\left(a\cos(x)+b\cos(Kx) \right)u_r(x;a,b,c_r) \right], \label{T2} T_{\omega,3} = F_{\omega}\left[u_r(x;a,b,c_r)^2 \right].\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-136: Wilton ripples represent a special class of small-amplitude, periodic, traveling-wave solutions that bifurcate from two co-propagating cosine waves.
elements.sent-2411-137: In general, these solutions represent important resonances in the study of water waves \cite{wilton1915lxxii as well as in magnetohydrodynamics \cite{zakaria2004wilton .}}
elements.sent-2411-138: Current existence proofs of Wilton ripples \cite{akers2021wiltonB -\cite{akers2021wiltonA , \cite{jones1989small, martin2013existence , and \cite{reeder1981wiltona -\cite{reeder1981wiltonb mostly consider the simplest resonant configuration, when the Wilton ripple bifurcates from a superposition of $\cos(x)$ and $\cos(2x)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-139: Among the primary challenges in proving the existence of the remaining Wilton ripples, which bifurcate from a superposition of $\cos(x)$ and $\cos(Kx)$ for any $K \in \mathbb{N \setminus \{1\}$, is to prove their analyticity with respect to a small-amplitude parameter.}
elements.sent-2411-140: This paper develops a proof that shows for the first time the existence of all Wilton ripple solutions of a nonlinear dispersive equation, namely the Kawahara equation.
elements.sent-2411-141: The proof relies on a carefully constructed Lyapunov-Schmidt reduction to show the analyticity of each solution with respect to a small-amplitude parameter.
elements.sent-2411-142: High-order asymptotic expansions of these solutions, derived in \cite{langer2024wilton , are then used to complete the proof.}
elements.sent-2411-143: The author anticipates that this proof can be extended to show the existence of all Wilton ripples of many other nonlinear dispersive equations, including those for gravity-capillary water waves, provided the necessary asymptotic expansions are computed.
elements.sent-2411-144: It is also expected that this proof will connect the classic approach to Wilton ripples, where equation parameters like $\beta$ are fixed, and more contemporary approaches that study sheets of Wilton ripples \cite{ehrnstrom2019bifurcation , where the superposition coefficients $a$ and $b$ are instead fixed.}
elements.sent-2411-145: We begin with the expansion of $u_r(x;a,b,c_r)$ when $K = 2$.
elements.sent-2411-146: Substituting \eqref{ur_exp into \eqref{Aeqn and equating collective powers in $a$, $b$, and $c_r$ yields a hierarchy of inhomogeneous differential equations that uniquely determines $u_r^{(i,j,k) (x)$ for increasingly larger $i$, $j$, and $k$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-147: Calculating these equations is straightforward but tedious, so we appeal to Mathematica for help.
elements.sent-2411-148: The interested reader can consult CompanionToKawaharaWiltonRipples.nb for further details.
elements.sent-2411-149: The differential equations for $u_r^{(i,j,k) (x)$ coincide when $i +j + k = 1$ and are given by \begin{equation}(c_0+L)u^{(i,j,k)}(x) = 0.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-150: As $u^{(i,j,k) (x) \in Null\{c_0+\mathcal{L}\}^\perp$, we conclude \begin{equation}u^{(i,j,k)}(x) = 0 \quad for \quad i+j+k =1. \label{urlin1}\end{equation} In other words, there are no first-order terms in the Taylor series expansion of $u_r(x;a,b,c_r)$.}
elements.sent-2411-151: When $i + j + k = 2$, we obtain six differential equations.
elements.sent-2411-153: We proceed as in Case 1 but leave the details to Mathematica.
elements.sent-2411-154: When $i + j + k = 1$, we find \begin{equation}u^{(i,j,k)}(x) = 0. \label{urlin2}\end{equation} When $i + j + k = 2$, we have instead \begin{subequations \begin{equation}u^{(2,0,0)}(x) = -\frac{1}{2c_0} - \frac{1}{2\left(c_0 - 4 + 16\beta \right)}\cos(2x) \label{u2_k} u^{(1,1,0)}(x) = -\frac{1}{c_0 - (K-1)^2 + \beta(K-1)^4}\cos((K-1)x) - \frac{1}{c_0 - (K+1)^2 + \beta(K+1)^4}\cos((K+1)x), \label{u11_k} u^{(0,2,0)}(x) = -\frac{1}{2c_0} - \frac{1}{2\left(c_0 - 4K^2 + 16\beta K^4 \right)}\cos(2Kx), \label{u02_k}\end{equation} \end{subequations and the remaining coefficient functions are zero.}}
elements.sent-2411-155: Remark: If $K = 2$, the coefficients of $\cos(2x)$ in \eqref{u2_k and of $\cos((K-1)x)$ in \eqref{u11_k are singular.}}
elements.sent-2411-156: This is to be expected, as $u^{(i,j,k) (x)$ must be orthogonal to $Null\{c_0+\mathcal{L}\} = Span\{\cos(x),\cos(2x)\}$ when $K = 2$.}
elements.sent-2411-157: It is for this reason that this case is considered separately.
